Summary:
Structure: causal chain.

PAX3-FOXO1 (P3F) functions as a pioneer transcription factor in fusion-positive rhabdomyosarcoma (FP-RMS) in mouse models and human TP53-deficient iPSC-derived endothelial cells. Mechanistically, the t(2;13) translocation joins PAX3 and FOXO1 regulatory regions to create novel TADs, enabling P3F to bind and open condensed chromatin, activate myogenic super-enhancers (including MYOD1, MYOGENIN, MYCN), and repress endothelial specification factors (e.g., FOXC2). Outcome: these activities are sufficient to transdifferentiate endothelial cells toward a myogenic program and to transform endothelial cells into FP-RMS tumors in vivo and in xenografts; continued P3F expression is required to maintain myogenic transcription factor expression and proliferative, tumorigenic capacity. FP-RMS tumors originating from endothelial cells are nearly indistinguishable from those originating from myogenic cells by histology, gene expression, and chromatin landscape.
References: Searcy, M. B., Larsen, R. K., Stevens, B. T., Zhang, Y., Jin, H., Drummond, C. J., Langdon, C. G., Gadek, K. E., Vuong, K., Reed, K. B., Garcia, M. R., Xu, B., Kimbrough, D. W., Adkins, G. E., Djekidel, N., Porter, S. N., Schreiner, P. A., Pruett-Miller, S. M., Abraham, B. J., … Hatley, M. E. (2023). PAX3-FOXO1 dictates myogenic reprogramming and rhabdomyosarcoma identity in endothelial progenitors. Nat Commun, 14(1), 7291. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-43044-1
